Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like it or when something was due soon. With my extracurriculars taking up time and not having a lot of money for extra resources, I mostly just listened to lecture recordings when I could fit them in between other things. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, other weeks barely anything.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and cram. It really depended on what else was going on in my life - work, activities, whatever. I'm not great at sticking to schedules even when I try to make them.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was due or I got an email notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since my attendance was pretty spotty. It wasn't really a habit, more like a reaction to realizing I probably should check in.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a couple weeks where I logged in more regularly, but it never became automatic for me. I think the peer support group I was connected with helped remind me to check things sometimes, which was actually pretty helpful when it happened.
Q5: The audio lectures and video content were definitely the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I'd often play them while doing other stuff or commuting. The discussion forums were okay too - I posted a bit and it helped me feel less lost when I saw other people had similar questions.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and any extra resources that required me to sit down and focus for long periods. With limited resources, I couldn't print things out, and reading on screens for hours just doesn't work for me. I'd skim them at best or just skip them entirely.
Q7: If I could tell it would directly help me pass an assignment or understand something I was confused about, I'd actually do it. Activities that felt like busywork or weren't clearly connected to my grades just didn't seem worth the effort. I needed to see the point immediately.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, I probably wasn't doing it unless I was genuinely curious, which wasn't often. Required activities at least had consequences attached, so I'd make myself do those even when I didn't want to.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I wasn't connected to wifi. I also borrowed a friend's notes sometimes since I missed so many classes. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over downloaded recordings or talking through concepts with people from my peer support group in person. None of that would show up anywhere. I also did a lot of thinking and processing while doing other activities - like I'd mentally review stuff while at work.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once and then listen to it multiple times over the next few days without ever logging back in. The LMS would just show one access, but I might have engaged with that content three or four times offline.
Q12: Midway through the semester things got really hard. I was stressed about money, my attendance had already dropped so much that going to class felt awkward, and I just started drifting. It wasn't that I didn't care exactly, I just couldn't seem to make myself show up consistently.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired from working or stressed about bills, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and deal with coursework. I'd tell myself I'd do it later and then later never came. Fatigue especially made me just want to zone out instead of study.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd log in and just scroll through stuff without really absorbing anything. I'd watch videos but my mind would be elsewhere completely. It felt like going through the motions just to feel like I was doing something, even though I wasn't really learning.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially readings and discussion posts. I knew they mattered but they felt overwhelming or boring, so I'd push them off. Sometimes I'd avoid logging in entirely because I didn't want to see how behind I was - it was easier to just not look.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd suddenly become super active right before something was due, cramming everything in at the last minute. Without a deadline looming, I had zero urgency. My whole engagement pattern was basically deadline-to-deadline sprints.
Q17: When I got feedback it was kind of surprising - sometimes better than I expected, sometimes worse. Good feedback made me feel like maybe I could actually do this, which helped a bit. Bad feedback just made me want to avoid thinking about the course for a while.
Q18: The structure was pretty flexible which sounds good but actually made it easier for me to drift. If there had been more check-ins or required attendance things, I might have stayed more connected. The openness meant I could disappear for weeks without anyone really noticing.
Q19: Having video and audio options made things easier for how I learn. But the amount of reading required was hard for me, and not having money for textbooks or printing made it worse. The peer support intervention actually helped because it gave me people to talk to about the course.
Q20: I think sometimes people are trying to figure things out or looking for what they need without actually being ready to produce work. Maybe they're stressed or confused or just procrastinating by browsing instead of doing. Activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That's kind of me, honestly. I didn't log in that much but I still passed because I studied in ways that didn't show up online. Listening to downloaded lectures, talking with peers, reviewing notes before exams. You can learn without being constantly logged in.
Q22: Logs can show when you accessed stuff and how often, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were just clicking around mindlessly. They definitely miss all the offline studying and the conversations and the thinking you do away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through motions to pass. It's when you're curious and want to learn, not just checking boxes. I wasn't always engaged even when I was active, and sometimes I was engaged even when I wasn't showing up.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually found the peer support really meaningful, or that I learned a lot from conversations that happened outside of class. It wouldn't show my stress about money or how that affected everything. It just sees clicks, not the whole messy picture of being a student.
